metadata.category: Bit Manipulation | Brute Force | Recursion | Memoization

content.problemStatement:
Chef has an array AAA of length NNN.

He can modify this array by applying a special operation **any** number of times. In one operation, he can:


Select two indices iii and jjj (1≤i<j≤∣A∣)(1\le i \lt j \le |A|)(1≤i<j≤∣A∣).
Append Ai⊕AjA_i \oplus A_jAi​⊕Aj​ to the end of the array, where ⊕\oplus⊕ denotes the bitwise XOR operation
Remove AiA_iAi​ and AjA_jAj​ from the array.
Chef wants to **maximize** the product of all the elements of the array after applying these operations.

Help Chef determine the **maximum product** he can achieve by applying this operation any (possibly zero) number of times. As this number can be large, print it modulo 998244353998244353998244353.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains one integer NNN — the number of elements in the array.
The second line consists of NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ denoting the elements of the array initially.




Output Format
For each test case, output the maximum product he can achieve modulo 998244353998244353998244353.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 150.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains an integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 15) – the size of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN (1 ≤ Ai ≤ 10^9).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the maximum possible product of the array elements after applying any number of the described operations, modulo 998244353.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Chef can make the following operations:

Operation 111: Choose i=1i = 1i=1 and j=2j = 2j=2, append A1⊕A2=1⊕2=3A_1\oplus A_2 = 1\oplus 2 = 3A1​⊕A2​=1⊕2=3 to the end of the array and remove elements A1A_1A1​ and A2A_2A2​. Thus, the array becomes [1,2,3][1, 2, 3][1,2,3].
Operation 222: Choose i=1i = 1i=1 and j=2j = 2j=2, append A1⊕A2=1⊕2=3A_1\oplus A_2 = 1\oplus 2 = 3A1​⊕A2​=1⊕2=3 to the end of the array and remove elements A1A_1A1​ and A2A_2A2​. Thus, the array becomes [3,3][3, 3][3,3].

The product of all elements of the array is 3×3=93\times 3 = 93×3=9. It can be shown that this is the maximum product that can be obtained by applying any number of operations on the array.
Test case 222: The product of all elements of the array is 3×3=93\times 3 = 93×3=9. It can be shown that this is the maximum product that can be obtained by applying any number of operations on the array.
Thus, Chef does not need to perform any operations.
Test case 333: Chef can make the following operation:

Operation 111: Choose i=1i = 1i=1 and j=2j = 2j=2, append A1⊕A2=1⊕2=3A_1\oplus A_2 = 1\oplus 2 = 3A1​⊕A2​=1⊕2=3 to the end of the array and remove elements A1A_1A1​ and A2A_2A2​. Thus, the array becomes [3][3][3].

The product of all elements is 333. It can be shown that this is the maximum product that can be obtained by applying any number of operations on the array.

solution.approach:
Because each operation reduces the size of the array by one, after at most N‑1 operations the array will contain a single element. For N ≤ 15 we can explore all possible sequences of operations. The state of the process is completely described by the multiset of current numbers. We store the numbers in a sorted tuple and use it as a key for memoisation. For a given state we have two possibilities:
1. Stop performing operations – the product of the numbers in the current state is a candidate answer.
2. Choose any unordered pair (i, j), replace Ai and Aj by Ai XOR Aj, sort the new list and recursively compute the best product for the new state.
The answer for the state is the maximum among the candidate of stopping and all recursive results. All products are computed with arbitrary‑precision integers (Python int, C++ boost::multiprecision::cpp_int) to avoid overflow; the final answer is taken modulo 998244353.
The total number of distinct states for N ≤ 15 is small enough (well below a few hundred thousand) to fit easily in time limits.
Time Complexity: O(number_of_states * N^2) – for each state we try O(N^2) pair merges. In practice this is well under 1 s for the given limits.
Space Complexity: O(number_of_states) for the memoisation table, each entry stores a big integer.
solution.timeComplexity: Approximately O(S * N^2), where S is the number of distinct states (S ≤ few × 10^5 for N ≤ 15).
